audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 11, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for siege setting, royal surveillance, Bathsheba's identity and washing, 'sent and took' coercion, pregnancy, attempted reproductive cover-up, intoxication, Uriah's loyalty, murder order, collateral deaths, command responsibility, mourning, and palace seizure.

verses.restored: At return of year, at time kings go out, David sent Joab, his servants with him, and all Israel. They ruined children of Ammon and besieged Rabbah, but David remained in Jerusalem.
At evening David arose from his bed and walked upon roof of house of king. From roof he saw a woman bathing, and woman was very good in appearance.
David sent and inquired concerning woman. One said, Is this not Bathsheba daughter of Eliam, wife of Uriah the Hittite?
David sent messengers and took her. She came to him, and he lay with her. She was cleansing herself from her impurity, and she returned to her house.
Woman conceived. She sent and told David, and said, I am pregnant.
David sent to Joab, saying, Send Uriah the Hittite to me. Joab sent Uriah to David.
Uriah came to him. David asked concerning peace of Joab, peace of people, and peace of war.
David said to Uriah, Go down to your house and wash your feet. Uriah went out from house of king, and a gift/portion from king went out after him.
Uriah lay at entrance of house of king with all servants of his lord and did not go down to his house.
They told David, saying, Uriah did not go down to his house. David said to Uriah, Did you not come from a journey? Why did you not go down to your house?

Uriah said to David, Ark, Israel, and Judah dwell in shelters; my lord Joab and servants of my lord camp upon face of field. Shall I go to my house to eat, drink, and lie with my wife? By your life and life of your soul, I will not do this thing.
David said to Uriah, Stay here also today, and tomorrow I will send you. Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and next.
David called him, and he ate and drank before him. David made him drunk. In evening he went out to lie on his bed with servants of his lord, but he did not go down to his house.
In morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it by hand of Uriah.
He wrote in letter, saying, Place Uriah at front of fiercest battle and turn back from behind him, so he may be struck and die.
When Joab watched city, he assigned Uriah to place where he knew strong warriors were.
Men of city went out and fought with Joab. Some of people, from servants of David, fell; Uriah the Hittite also died.
Joab sent and told David all matters of war.
He commanded messenger, saying, When you finish speaking all matters of war to king,
if anger of king rises and he says, Why did you approach so near city to fight? Did you not know they would shoot from wall?

Who struck Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Did not a woman throw upper millstone upon him from wall so he died at Thebez? Why did you approach wall? Then say, Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.
Messenger went, came, and told David all that Joab had sent him to tell.
Messenger said to David, Men were strong against us and came out to us into field, but we pressed them as far as entrance of gate.
Shooters shot at your servants from wall. Some servants of king died, and your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.
David said to messenger, Thus say to Joab: Do not let this thing be evil in your eyes, because sword devours one and another. Strengthen your battle against city and overthrow it. Strengthen him.
Wife of Uriah heard that Uriah her husband was dead, and she lamented over her husband.
When mourning passed, David sent and gathered her to his house. She became his wife and bore him a son. But thing David had done was evil in eyes of YHWH.

translation_notes: Bathsheba is named before the encounter as daughter of Eliam and wife of Uriah. Her relationships and personhood precede David's desire.

The Hebrew sequence says David 'sent' and 'took' her. A king with messengers, palace power, and authority over her husband creates coercion; the text records no request from David and no consent from Bathsheba.
Her ritual washing is not seduction. The rooftop viewpoint belongs to David; she is not blamed for being seen.
Her message 'I am pregnant' is the chapter's clearest direct speech and exposes consequences that royal power tries to conceal.
David attempts reproductive cover-up by ordering Uriah home, then uses alcohol when the first plan fails. Intoxication is an instrument of coercion here.
Uriah is a Hittite and a loyal soldier; ethnicity does not make him less part of Israel or less worthy of justice.

David sends Uriah carrying his own death order and engineers battlefield abandonment. Joab executes the plan, and additional soldiers die. This is murder through command authority and collateral killing.
'The sword devours one and another' is David's euphemism for a death he arranged. Fatalism must not erase command responsibility.
Bathsheba's mourning is centered. Palace marriage after the murder does not retroactively create consent or repair the abuse.
safety_note: Maximum survivor-centered safeguards: This chapter depicts royal sexual abuse, reproductive coercion, intoxication, cover-up, murder, and war-command abuse. Do not call Bathsheba a temptress or adulteress, speculate that she invited David's gaze, or use later marriage to erase coercion. David's spiritual importance never exempts him from accountability.
